data.title: Trump tariff decisions influenced by 'last person to whisper' in his ear - Ritholtz Wealth
data.description:
According to investment advisory firm Ritholtz Wealth Management, tariff decisions by the current U.S. administration are influenced by "the last person to whisper" in President Donald Trump's ear.

Ritholtz's comments came after Trump earlier in the day reignited trade war concerns with threats of aggressive tariffs on Apple (AAPL) and the European Union (EU). Trump's remarks represented a sharp escalation in trade developments after nearly two weeks of calm sparked by a surprise truce between the U.S. and China.

"A simple strategy to figure out what the next tariff policy decision will be from this White House: Who is the last person to whisper in POTUS' ear before a major decision is made? Every other input matters much less than whoever is the key administration member who most influences major trade policy decisions," Barry Ritholtz, chairman and chief investment officer at Ritholtz Wealth Management, said on X (formerly Twitter) on Friday.

U.S. stocks have sold off on Friday on Trump's new threats, as investors grapple with the implications of the President's recommended 50% straight tariff on the EU's goods. Meanwhile, Apple (AAPL) shares are -2.7%.      

Ritholtz believes that markets will be better suited if Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent influenced trade decisions, rather than Peter Navarro, the Senior Counselor for Trade and Manufacturing to the President.

"If it's Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent -- Yale graduate, former partner at Soros Fund Management, founder of global macro investment firm Key Square Group -- then expect the market to behave fairly normally, with traditional inputs and responses. If Bessent influences 75% of trade decisions, the market will do just fine," Ritholtz said.

"If it's trade advisor Peter Navarro (Harvard JFK School of Government), then the outcome will be different. Navarro is one of the only policy wonks in the world who believes tariffs are a useful economic tool to raise revenue and spur economic growth. If Navarro influences 75% of trade decisions, then buckle up! We are in for a very bumpy ride," he added. 

The U.S. currently has in place a blanket 10% tariff on all trading partners except China, and has instituted a 90-day pause on higher proposed reciprocal tariffs. U.S. tariffs on Chinese goods have been temporarily stayed at 30%.

Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU). 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

More on tariffs and trade:

  • Apple dives as Trump says tariffs must be paid on iPhones not made in US
  • Stock index futures fall sharply, yields slip as Trump threatens tariffs on Apple, EU
  • Trump threatens 50% tariff on EU products starting in June
  • Apple suppliers see red after Trump says tech giant must pay tariffs on iPhones not made in US
  • Trump's tariff threat at Apple is 'frustrating situation for investors,' Wedbush says

data.title: Tariff tensions rattle European auto stocks
data.description:
President Trump’s response to a lack of progress in trade talks with the European Union is reverberating through the automobile sector as shares of carmakers Ferrari (NYSE:RACE), Stellantis (NYSE:STLA), Mercedes-Benz (OTCPK:MBGAF) (OTCPK:MBGYY), Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY) (OTCPK:VLKAF) (OTCPK:VWAPY), Porsche (OTCPK:POAHY), and BMW (OTC:BMWKY) (OTCPK:BAMXF) underperform their U.S. rivals.

On Friday, Trump threatened a 50% tariff on goods manufactured in the EU amid frustrations with the difficulty in hammering out a deal.

“Our discussions with them are going nowhere!” Trump said in a post on Truth Social, echoed by Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent.

“EU proposals have not been of the same quality that we’ve seen from our other important trading partners,” Bessent said, adding that the EU “has a collective action problem.”

Europe’s major bourses all retreated on the development, dragging down shares of the major automakers, with Mercedes-Benz (OTCPK:MBGAF)(OTCPK:MBGYY) taking the brunt on Germany’s DAX. While the company sells more than a third of its vehicles in China, the U.S. follows with a 16% share, and Germany at 10%. Mercedes-Benz shares shed 4% on Friday.

Stellantis (NYSE:STLA) stands out as the most diversified among EU-domiciled auto manufacturers with facilities in the U.S., Canada, France, Italy, Germany, and the UK, along with a presence in Brazil. That didn’t stop investors from bailing out of the stock, driving STLA down 3.7% versus a more modest decline of less than 1% in shares of U.S. rivals General Motors (GM) and Ford (F).

Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY)(OTCPK:VLKAF)(OTCPK:VWAPY) is almost as diversified as Stellantis (NYSE:STLA) with its largest plant at its Wolfsburg, Germany headquarters. The company also maintains a significant manufacturing presence in the U.S., with production of the Passat, Atlas, and all-electric ID.4 in Chattanooga, Tennessee. Unfortunately, because of soft demand for EVs, Volkswagen has reduced shifts and scaled back production. The stock closed 3% lower in Germany, setting a one-month low.

data.title: Perpetua submits application for up to $2B loan to U.S. Export-Import Bank for antimony project
data.description:
Perpetua Resources (NASDAQ:PPTA) +4.2% in Friday's trading after saying it submitted a formal application to the U.S. Export-Import Bank for potential debt financing of as much as $2B to construct its Stibnite antimony and gold project in Idaho.

The company had received a letter of interest from the EXIM Bank in 2024 for a loan of as much as $1.8B for the project, and it said the increase in the application amount reflects an increase in the estimated number of job-years indicated by the financial update and basic engineering work completed in Q1.

The Stibnite mine, which is backed by the Trump administration and would be the first U.S. antimony project, contains an estimated reserve of 148M lbs of the metal used in bullets and tanks, as well as in alloys for electric vehicle batteries.

Earlier this week, Perpetua received the final federal permit - the Clean Water Act Section 404 - from the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers for the project.

data.title: White House AI czar says EUV lithography China restrictions to continue
data.description:
David Sacks, the White House artificial intelligence and cryptocurrency czar, said on Friday that restricting the sale of extreme lithography machines to China must continue.

“Restricting the sale of EUV lithography equipment to China is the single most important export control that we have in the area of semiconductors,” Sacks wrote in a post on the social network X. “The first Trump administration began the restriction, and it’s of paramount importance that it continues.”

Extreme ultraviolet lithography machines are manufactured by Dutch semiconductor equipment manufacturer ASML (NASDAQ:ASML). ASML shares fell 1.5% in midday trading on Friday.

Sacks' comments come after the China government had asked the Netherlands a second time to relax its semiconductor export restrictions, according to Bloomberg, citing comments made by Dutch Foreign Affairs Minister Caspar VeldKamp. China expressed dissatisfaction with the export control curbs in September 2024.

ASML was allowed to ship chip equipment machines to China until the end of 2023 under the export control curbs.

Both the Netherlands and Japan joined the U.S. in curbing exports of certain semiconductors and chip-making equipment to China, amid national security concerns.

data.title: Fed's Cook says Treasury markets held up during April's tariff-induced volatility
data.description:
Federal Reserve Board Governor Lisa Cook said Friday that last month's financial market volatility, sparked by U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff announcement, did not lead to the same kind of market dysfunction seen at the onset of the Covid-19 pandemic.

In early April, when Trump said he will implement massive tariff hikes on trading partners, Treasury markets functioned "in an orderly fashion throughout the episode," Cook said in a speech prepared for delivery at the Seventh Annual Women in Macro Conference.

Cook added that "market depth and other liquidity measures decreased from already low levels, but the decline was in line with what would be anticipated, given the elevated volatility in markets."

She said corporate earnings calls and Fed outreach pointed to growing concern over economic uncertainty, as well as slower growth and rising inflation. Still, she noted, some of that fear has eased since mid-April, as Trump made a U-turn on tariffs (until today). 

"The episode provided a real-life example of the large asset-price declines and sudden bursts of volatility that can result from shocks when asset valuations are stretched, as well as the importance of stable and resilient funding markets in absorbing shocks," Cook explained.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: SA analyst rating changes: PANW, SNOW, AAP, EXR, INTA
data.description:
Here are the latest stock rating changes by Seeking Alpha analysts:

Upgrades:

Palo Alto Networks (NASDAQ:PANW). Hold to buy by Vinay Utham. "I am upgrading Palo Alto Networks from Hold to Buy, citing strong Q3 results, robust guidance, and a promising AI cybersecurity strategy…Despite macro risks, valuation offers 27% upside, and PANW is well-positioned to benefit from the ongoing AI-driven cybersecurity demand."

Extra Space Storage (NYSE:EXR). Hold to buy by Seeking Profits. "At $145, EXR offers a secure dividend and attractive go-forward income growth prospects. I believe its period of underperformance is coming to an end, and investors can begin adding shares."

Downgrades:

Snowflake (NYSE:SNOW). Buy to hold by JR Research. "Valuation risk is significant, with SNOW trading at 110x forward EBITDA, leaving little margin for error if growth or execution falters. Learning my lessons from SNOW's almost 40% tumble from its February peak, I caution investors about attempting to chase the post-earnings spike."

Advance Auto Parts (NYSE:AAP). Hold to sell by Ian Bezek. "I am not convinced that the company will be able to meet its 2025 guidance given weak margins, negative sales trends, and past lack of execution. At current prices near $48, the risk/reward is unattractive; I downgrade AAP stock to a Sell."

Intapp (NASDAQ:INTA). Buy to hold by Gary Alexander. "I view Intapp to be a relatively high quality, somewhat high-priced software stock. In a bull market, a stock positioned like this may have the capacity to rally sharply, but my macro view is that we'll see a minor correction in the order of ~10% that disproportionately hits richly valued growth stocks such as Intapp, making me hesitant to hold on to my position."

data.title: TotalEnergies CEO defends company against abuse allegations in African project
data.description:
TotalEnergies (NYSE:TTE) CEO Patrick Pouyanne defended the company's work on oil projects in east Africa at its annual shareholders meeting Friday, Reuters reported, after a United Nations human rights representative said the company needs to address fresh allegations of abuses.

Special Rapporteur on Environmental Defenders Michel Forst said TotalEnergies (NYSE:TTE) had failed to take effective steps to address abuses against activists linked to the $15B East African crude oil pipeline project and connected oilfields.

Also, Germany's Union Investment said it dropped French company from its sustainability funds and called for an independent human rights audit following abuse allegations related to the project.

TotalEnergies (TTE) denied the allegations concerning the Kingfisher oil site in Uganda, part of the East African crude oil pipeline project in which the company holds a 62% stake.

"We develop these projects with the values and principles that are ours, we are convinced of the good they do for the countries and the local populations," Pouyanne said, adding that projects such as the UGanda site are better done by TotalEnergies (TTE) than others, who may care less about human rights and the environment.

data.title: Markets aren't priced for a slowdown - Fidelity Investments
data.description:
The markets are not pricing a significant slowdown despite the volatility seen due to economic policy, according to Jurrien Timmer, director of global macro at Fidelity Investments.

During a CNBC interview, Timmer suggested that the markets have established boundaries, where there appears to be a “pain point which will not be exceeded” based on previous experience when “some of the more aggressive tariff talk was rolled back” after market declines.

He predicted 2025 would see moderate earnings growth “offset by value pressures coming mostly from the interest rate side.”

Chicago Federal Reserve President Austan Goolsbee said tariff changes could create a “stagflationary impact” that would slow economic output while raising prices. He described this as “the central bank’s worst situation” where “both sides of the legal mandate get worse at the same time.”

Timmer explained that tariffs function essentially as a tax that must be paid by someone — either companies through lower profit margins or consumers through higher prices.

“Clearly, earnings estimates have been marked down, not significantly worse than what you typically see during the year, but they have been marked down as have the GDP estimates,” he said.

The market has experienced significant volatility, with Timmer noting it “went down 21.5% very quickly, and then we recovered 23% very quickly.” Despite this recovery, he believes the market is now constrained by dual pressures: “It’s either going to be rates driving down valuations or earnings driving down the estimates from tariffs.”

In addition, the competitive nature of treasury yields (US10Y), (US2Y), (NASDAQ:GOVI), (NYSEARCA:SPTL), (NYSEARCA:SPTI) against equity (SP500), (DJI), (COMP:IND) returns further complicates the outlook, according to Timmer.

“The 10-year Treasury (US10Y) at 4.5% is quite competitive with the equity yield also at around 4.5-5%,” he explained, adding that rising yields due to fiscal concerns would affect valuations while tariff-related economic slowdowns could risk earnings estimates.

“Either way, I think there’s a little bit of a ceiling here on the markets,” Timmer concluded.

data.title: Apple CEO reaches out to Texas governor to amend online child-safety legislation: report
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) CEO Tim Cook allegedly reached out to Texas Gov. Greg Abbot recently, asking him to amend proposed legislation that would require age verification and mandate parental consent before a minor can download any apps or make purchases within apps on smartphones, according to the Wall Street Journal. 

Cook asked Abbot to amend the legislation, or even veto it if that fails, according to the report, which cited people familiar with the matter.

The Texas legislature has already passed the legislation, Senate Bill 2420. It was sent to the governor's desk on May 15. 

"Growing concerns regarding the rise of social media and its pervasiveness in the lives of children and teens leave parents in the position of grasping for the best ways to protect their children," according to the bill. "Unlike brick and mortar stores which must verify a consumer's age before the purchase of age restricted products such as alcohol and cigarettes, minors are currently able to navigate through the digital world without such parameters."

"The App Store Accountability Act remedies this by requiring app stores to gain consent from parents to consent to the use of mobile applications by their minor children and, additionally, to provide information from the app developers regarding the app's rating and the reasoning for the rating," it adds. "App stores have touted that they already employ age verification, so this simply provides additional framework, transparency, and enforcement to protect the children of Texas."

Several lobbying groups representing the tech industry have opposed the bill, arguing that it places too much burden on companies running app stores, such as Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) and Google (NASDAQ:GOOG)(NASDAQ:GOOGL). They also cite privacy rights and the challenges in confirming the age of users.

"The bill would require platforms to use tools such as facial age estimation or to collect sensitive personal information such as driver's licenses," according to the Computer and Communications Industry Association. "But experts have noted that facial estimation technology can produce inconsistent results across demographics, and collecting additional data raises significant privacy concerns for families. Age verification requirements can also restrict access for users who cannot easily verify their age, including older teens without IDs or individuals from underserved communities."

"The fact that every mobile device user would have to go through the age verification process to use the app stores also almost certainly dooms this bill on constitutional grounds," according to testimony from R Street. "Previous attempts to enact broad age-gating restrictions for online services have been found to violate the First Amendment in the past."  

Another bill cruising through the Texas legislature is House Bill 186. It would ban all Texans under the age of 18 from creating social media accounts, which would affect social media companies such as Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) and ByteDance (BDNCE). It has already passed the house with broad bipartisan support.

So far, nine states have passed some kind of law restricting minors' access to social media, with these usually requiring parental consent, according to The Age Verification Providers Association. Florida is the only state with an outright ban, but it only applies to minors under the age of 14. Texas is the second most populous state in the U.S. so any meaningful legislation in this area could produce significant effects. 

Seeking Alpha reached out to Apple for comment.

data.title: Wall Street slides as Trump threatens Apple & the European Union with tariffs
data.description:
Wall Street fell on Friday, as sentiment was slammed by U.S. President Donald Trump's threats to impose tariffs on tech giant Apple (AAPL) and the European Union (EU).     

The benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) was last -0.67% at 5,802.84 points in midday trade, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.57% at 41,622.09 points. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.94% at 18,747.83 points.

The impact of Trump's announcements on his Truth Social service early in the morning was immediate, with stock index futures plunging. 

First, Trump took aim at Apple (AAPL). He pitched a tariff of at least 25% on the tech giant's products if it did not build its iPhones in the U.S. Apple (AAPL) shares were last -2.6%.

"The pressure from Trump on Apple to build iPhone production in the U.S. as we have discussed this would result in an iPhone price point that is a non-starter for Cupertino and translate into iPhone prices of ~$3,500 if it was made in the U.S. which is not realistic in our view," Wedbush's Dan Ives said on X (formerly Twitter). 

Then, Trump targeted the EU. After pointing out several of the EU's "unfair" practices, he asserted that trade discussions with the union were "going nowhere" and that he was recommending a straight 50% tariff on EU goods starting June 1.   

The EU is one of the biggest trading partners of the United States. According to the U.S. Trade Representative, total goods trade with the European Union were an estimated $975.9B in 2024.

Charlie Bilello, chief market strategist at Creative Planning, advised market participants that the EU tariff recommendation should not be taken at "face value."

"Goes without saying but no one should take any of these hyperbolic statements at face value as we've learned again and again over the past few months. There is no scenario where 50% tariffs on the EU would last for very long because the markets would revolt and a deal to lower them would quickly be reached," Bilello said on X.

But others expressed frustration over Trump's actions. 

"On again, off again, deals, no deals, 10 percent, 30 percent, 145 percent, 50 percent…how is any American business supposed to plan in this environment?" Tim Chapman, president at Advancing American Freedom, a public policy advocacy organization founded by former Vice President Mike Pence, said on X.

"Tariffs are a dial, not an on-off switch. Stocks go up, (Trump) may feel like he has license to turn the dial back on. Stocks go down enough, the opposite is true," Renaissance Macro Research said on X.

Turning to the fixed-income markets, U.S. Treasury yields continued to stabilize following a bruising bond sell-off on Wednesday. The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) was last down 4 basis points to 4.50%, while the shorter-end more rate-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y) was down 1 basis point to 3.99%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.
